ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for rice flakes as a distinct food product; SNOMED CT coverage for processed grain products is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical dry serving of rice flakes is approximately 40 g (about 4–5 tablespoons), which expands when soaked or cooked.

nutrient_source.title: Rice flakes — Singapore Health Promotion Board Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values for energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, calcium, iron, and cholesterol taken directly from HPB Foodo record for rice flakes (cr_id F05090014). Secondary nutrients (dietary fibre, sodium, sugar, saturated fat) supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for rice flakes (NDB No. 20051) where HPB does not report those fields.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for generic rice flakes found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 85 is estimated from the closest analogous entry: 'Rice, white, flaked' reported in Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283), which lists GI ~85 (glucose reference). Glycemic load per serving calculated as (85 × 40 g serving × 76.03 g carb per 100 g) / 100 = 25.8.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (~10 mg/100 g) with no added salt in plain rice flakes
hypertension.guidance: Plain rice flakes are acceptable for hypertension management due to their very low sodium content (~10 mg/100 g); however, patients should verify packaging for added salt and avoid preparing with high-sodium condiments or sauces.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycaemic index (~85) and high carbohydrate load (76 g/100 g) with low dietary fibre (~1.5 g/100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Rice flakes are not recommended for Type 2 diabetes management due to their high GI (~85) and high carbohydrate density (76 g/100 g) with minimal fibre; if consumed, limit dry portion to 30 g and combine with protein or legumes to attenuate postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat and zero cholesterol, but high refined carbohydrate load may raise triglycerides
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Rice flakes are low in saturated fat (0.6 g/100 g) and cholesterol-free, which is favourable for cardiovascular disease; however, their high refined carbohydrate content (76 g/100 g) and low fibre may contribute to elevated triglycerides and should be consumed in controlled portions as part of a balanced diet.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High glycaemic load from refined carbohydrate promotes pro-inflammatory mediators; no meaningful anti-inflammatory nutrients present
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Rice flakes are not recommended for rheumatoid arthritis due to their high glycaemic load and absence of anti-inflammatory nutrients (omega-3, polyphenols, significant whole-food fibre); if consumed, pair with anti-inflammatory foods such as walnuts, flaxseed, or berries.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Rice flakes (also known as flattened rice, beaten rice, or poha) are a processed grain product made from parboiled or raw rice that has been husked, parboiled, flattened between rollers, and dried. The result is thin, white, crisp flakes with a mild, neutral rice flavour and a light, airy texture that softens quickly when soaked in water or milk. Rice flakes are a staple ingredient across South Asia and Southeast Asia, used in breakfast dishes, snacks, and festival foods. In India, they are the primary ingredient in poha (a spiced breakfast dish), chivda (a dry snack mix), and various sweet preparations. In Singapore and Malaysia, they appear in rice-based porridges and as a breakfast cereal. Nutritionally, rice flakes are energy-dense (351 kcal/100 g) and carbohydrate-dominant (76 g/100 g) with moderate protein (7.3 g/100 g), low fat (2.07 g/100 g), and low dietary fibre (~1.5 g/100 g). They are naturally gluten-free, cholesterol-free, and very low in sodium (~10 mg/100 g). The iron content is notably high (6.77 mg/100 g), partly due to iron fortification in commercial products. The glycaemic index is high (~85), making rice flakes a rapid-digestion carbohydrate source that is unsuitable for Type 2 diabetes management without portion control and protein pairing. They are appropriate for hypertension due to low sodium, but their refined carbohydrate profile warrants moderation for cardiovascular and inflammatory conditions.
common_user_queries: Is poha the same as rice flakes?
How many calories in rice flakes?
What is the GI of rice flakes?
Rice flakes vs oats — which is healthier?
Are rice flakes good for weight loss?

confidence_reason: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, calcium, iron, cholesterol) sourced directly from HPB Foodo database (cr_id F05090014, confidence 0.95). Secondary nutrients (dietary fibre, sodium, sugar, saturated fat, B-vitamins, minerals) supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for rice flakes, which is an approved primary source. GI estimated from the closest analogous entry in the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008 peer-reviewed publication.
notes: 1. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero per HPB Foodo source; rice flakes are a plant-derived product containing no cholesterol. 2. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — rice flakes are a minimally processed plant grain product; trans fat is not present. 3. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — plant-derived food; vitamin B12 is naturally absent. 4. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 — no significant vitamin D in rice flakes; confirmed by USDA SR Legacy. 5. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 — negligible vitamin C in processed rice flakes per USDA SR Legacy. 6. vitamin_a_mcg_rae set to -1: not reported in HPB Foodo or USDA SR Legacy for plain rice flakes. 7. vitamin_e_mg set to -1: not reported in HPB Foodo; USDA SR Legacy does not provide a reliable value for this product form. 8. selenium_mcg set to -1: not reported in HPB Foodo; limited published data for this specific product form. 9. GI of 85 is an analogous estimate from Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care) for 'rice, white, flaked'; no direct measurement for generic rice flakes found in the University of Sydney GI Database as of April 2026. 10. Dietary fibre (~1.5 g/100 g), sodium (~10 mg/100 g), and potassium (~120 mg/100 g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB No. 20051, rice, white, long-grain, regular, raw) as the closest approved-source equivalent; actual values may vary by brand and processing method. 11. Iron value of 6.77 mg/100 g from HPB is notably high and may reflect iron fortification in commercial rice flakes sold in Singapore; consumers should verify with product packaging.
